meta.title: Lekhio law exam bank
meta.note: The legal sibling of test/exams. Where the accounting bank proves our tax ENGINE against a number, this proves our legal KNOWLEDGE against the primary source: every question is anchored to a statute section or a named case, on a host we are licensed to cite (see lib/lawsources.ts). Answers are kept QUALITATIVE and pinned to a provision, never a bare threshold, because a number can be amended under us and the point of Khoji is to catch that, not to enshrine it. Coverage is counted honestly: 'rule' = cross-checked against lib/rulesources.ts, 'knowledge' = we hold the source and could answer, 'uncovered' = a gap we admit and count. Drawn from the syllabus TOPICS of the professional exams in lib/lawsources.ts; no past-paper text is reproduced.
meta.discipline: Broad to examine ourselves on. Narrow to assert to a user. Nothing in this file is an answer sent to anyone; it is how we test that we are right.

questions.prompt: A self-employed electrician buys ordinary trousers and a shirt worn only for work. Deductible against trading profit?
questions.answer: No. Ordinary clothing fails the wholly-and-exclusively test because it also serves the everyday human need for warmth and decency.

questions.prompt: What is the statutory test for whether a trading expense is deductible for income tax?
questions.answer: It must be incurred wholly and exclusively for the purposes of the trade.

questions.prompt: A company with no distributable profits pays a dividend. What is the position?
questions.answer: The distribution is unlawful; a member who knew or had reasonable grounds to know is liable to repay it.

questions.prompt: Where are a director's core general duties codified?
questions.answer: In the seven general duties, including to promote the success of the company and to exercise reasonable care, skill and diligence.

questions.prompt: What ongoing filing confirms a company's information to Companies House at least annually?
questions.answer: The confirmation statement (which replaced the annual return).

questions.prompt: Which statute and part govern an ordinary unfair dismissal claim, and where is the qualifying period set?
questions.answer: Part X of the Employment Rights Act 1996; the qualifying period of continuous employment is set by s108. (The length is a live figure Khoji watches, not enshrined here.)
questions.authority: Part X and s108 Employment Rights Act 1996

questions.prompt: Name a reason for dismissal that is automatically unfair with no qualifying period.
questions.answer: Dismissal for making a protected disclosure (whistleblowing), or for a reason connected with pregnancy or maternity.
questions.authority: s103A and s99 Employment Rights Act 1996

questions.prompt: How is an employee's statutory minimum notice period structured?
questions.answer: One week after one month's service, then one additional week per complete year up to a statutory maximum of twelve weeks.

questions.prompt: Under what statute are the protected characteristics for discrimination defined?
questions.answer: The Equality Act 2010, which lists nine protected characteristics including age, disability, sex, and race.

questions.prompt: A tradesman sells goods to a consumer. What statutory quality standard must the goods meet?
questions.answer: They must be of satisfactory quality, fit for particular purpose, and as described.

questions.prompt: A consumer receives faulty goods. What immediate remedy does the Consumer Rights Act give?
questions.answer: A short-term right to reject the goods and obtain a refund, exercisable within the statutory period.
questions.authority: s20 and s22 Consumer Rights Act 2015

questions.topic: Services with reasonable care and skill
questions.prompt: What standard must a trader meet when supplying a service to a consumer?
questions.answer: The service must be performed with reasonable care and skill.

questions.prompt: What is the limitation period for an action founded on a simple contract?
questions.answer: Six years from the date the cause of action accrued.

questions.prompt: What is the limitation period for a personal injury claim in negligence?
questions.answer: Three years from the date of the injury or the date of knowledge, if later.

questions.prompt: Which case established the modern neighbour principle for a duty of care in negligence?
questions.answer: Donoghue v Stevenson, establishing that one owes a duty to persons so closely and directly affected that one ought reasonably to have them in contemplation.

questions.prompt: Does a photographer need to register a photograph to hold copyright in it?
questions.answer: No. Copyright arises automatically on creation of a qualifying original work; there is no registration system for copyright in the UK.
questions.authority: s1 Copyright, Designs and Patents Act 1988

questions.prompt: How long does copyright in a literary, dramatic, musical or artistic work last?
questions.answer: For the life of the author plus 70 years from the end of the year of death.
questions.authority: s12 Copyright, Designs and Patents Act 1988

questions.prompt: A tradesman keeps customer contact details. What must they have to process that personal data?
questions.answer: A lawful basis for processing, such as consent, contract, or legitimate interests.
questions.authority: Article 6 UK GDPR, as applied by the Data Protection Act 2018

questions.prompt: A customer asks a business for a copy of the personal data it holds on them. What is this and what is the response deadline anchored to?
questions.answer: A subject access request; the controller must respond without undue delay and within the statutory period set by the UK GDPR.
questions.authority: Article 15 UK GDPR, Data Protection Act 2018

questions.prompt: What is the primary statutory duty of an employer regarding the health and safety of employees?
questions.answer: To ensure, so far as is reasonably practicable, the health, safety and welfare at work of all employees.
questions.authority: s2 Health and Safety at Work etc. Act 1974

questions.prompt: Does a self-employed builder owe a health and safety duty to members of the public affected by the work?
questions.answer: Yes. There is a duty to conduct the undertaking so that persons not in your employment are not exposed to risks to their health or safety, so far as is reasonably practicable.
questions.authority: s3 Health and Safety at Work etc. Act 1974

questions.prompt: A dispute arises under a construction contract. What dispute mechanism does the party have a statutory right to?
questions.answer: A right to refer the dispute to adjudication at any time.
questions.authority: s108 Housing Grants, Construction and Regeneration Act 1996

questions.prompt: A director continues trading when they knew there was no reasonable prospect of avoiding insolvent liquidation. What is the exposure?
questions.answer: Potential personal liability to contribute to the company's assets for wrongful trading.

questions.prompt: In a short residential lease, who is responsible for repairing the structure and exterior, and the supply of water, gas and electricity?
questions.answer: The landlord, under an implied repairing covenant that cannot be contracted out of.

questions.prompt: A tradesperson takes on their first employee. When must a written statement of the main employment terms be given?
questions.answer: On or before the first day of employment. The principal statement is a day-one right, pinned to the provision rather than to any current list of particulars.

questions.prompt: An employee is dismissed. What continuous-service condition must ordinarily be met before an ordinary unfair dismissal claim can be brought?
questions.answer: A qualifying period of continuous employment (currently two years) is generally required, save for automatically-unfair reasons which need no qualifying period. The requirement is set by the provision, which is what we watch.

questions.prompt: How is the statutory minimum notice an employer must give an employee determined?
questions.answer: By length of continuous service: one week once a month has passed, then one week for each complete year, up to a statutory maximum. The scheme is fixed by the provision, not by us.

questions.prompt: On what basis can a dismissal be defended as fair?
questions.answer: It must be for one of the potentially fair reasons (capability, conduct, redundancy, statutory bar or some other substantial reason) and the employer must have acted reasonably in treating it as sufficient.

questions.prompt: What service condition must an employee meet to be entitled to a statutory redundancy payment?
questions.answer: A qualifying period of continuous employment (currently two years) ending with the relevant date. The condition lives in the provision.

questions.prompt: Under the Equality Act, discrimination is unlawful only where it relates to a protected characteristic. Where are those characteristics defined?
questions.answer: In the Act itself, which lists the protected characteristics (age, disability, gender reassignment, marriage and civil partnership, pregnancy and maternity, race, religion or belief, sex, and sexual orientation).

questions.prompt: What is direct discrimination?
questions.answer: Treating a person less favourably than others are or would be treated, because of a protected characteristic.

questions.prompt: What is a worker's statutory paid annual leave entitlement?
questions.answer: Paid annual leave (currently 5.6 weeks in total, combining the basic and additional entitlements) provided by the Working Time Regulations, which is the instrument we watch for change.

questions.prompt: An employer pays a worker below the national minimum wage for their age. What is the position?
questions.answer: Unlawful. A worker of the qualifying age must be paid at least the applicable National Minimum or National Living Wage rate, with arrears and penalties for underpayment.
questions.authority: GOV.UK National Minimum Wage rates (National Minimum Wage Act 1998)

questions.prompt: A customer refuses to pay an invoice for completed work. How long is there to sue on the contract?
questions.answer: Six years from the date the cause of action accrued, for an action founded on a simple contract. The period is set by the provision.

questions.prompt: How does the limitation period differ where the obligation is contained in a deed rather than a simple contract?
questions.answer: An action on a specialty (a contract by deed) has a longer limitation period, currently twelve years from accrual, under the provision.

questions.prompt: Within what period must an action in tort for damage to property generally be brought?
questions.answer: Six years from the date the cause of action accrued, for actions founded on tort (other than personal injury, which is shorter). The period is fixed by the provision.

questions.prompt: A worker is injured on site. What is the ordinary limitation period for a personal injury claim?
questions.answer: Three years, running from the date of the injury or the claimant's date of knowledge, whichever is later, subject to the court's discretion to extend. The period sits in the provision.

questions.prompt: How do the courts decide whether a duty of care exists in a novel negligence situation?
questions.answer: By the threefold test of reasonable foreseeability of harm, a relationship of sufficient proximity, and whether it is fair, just and reasonable to impose a duty.
questions.authority: Caparo Industries plc v Dickman [1990] 2 AC 605 (HL)

questions.prompt: A tradesperson supplies goods to a consumer as part of a job. What quality standard is implied?
questions.answer: That the goods are of satisfactory quality, judged by what a reasonable person would regard as satisfactory, taking account of description, price and other relevant circumstances. It is a statutory term that cannot be excluded against a consumer.

questions.topic: Services with reasonable care and skill
questions.prompt: What standard does a consumer service contract imply about how the work is done?
questions.answer: That the trader will perform the service with reasonable care and skill. It is a term treated as included in every consumer service contract and cannot be excluded to the consumer's detriment.

questions.prompt: No price was agreed for a job before it was done. What price is a consumer obliged to pay?
questions.answer: A reasonable price, and no more, where the price has not been fixed by the contract or a course of dealing. Reasonableness is a question of fact under the provision.

questions.prompt: No completion date was agreed. By when must a consumer service be performed?
questions.answer: Within a reasonable time, treated as a term of the contract where no time is fixed. What is reasonable is a question of fact.

questions.prompt: Goods supplied to a consumer are faulty. Is there a short window in which they can simply reject them?
questions.answer: Yes. A consumer has a short-term right to reject non-conforming goods (currently within 30 days) and obtain a refund, in addition to later rights to repair, replacement or price reduction. The window is set by the provisions.

questions.prompt: A dispute arises on a construction contract over a payment. What quick dispute route does either party have?
questions.answer: A statutory right to refer the dispute to adjudication at any time, giving a decision that binds until finally determined by litigation, arbitration or agreement.
questions.authority: s108 Housing Grants, Construction and Regeneration Act 1996

questions.prompt: A construction contract for work lasting more than 45 days is silent on payment timing. Is the contractor entitled to interim payment?
questions.answer: Yes. There is a statutory right to payment by instalments, stage or periodic payments for such contracts; if the contract does not provide an adequate mechanism, the Scheme for Construction Contracts applies.
questions.authority: s109 Housing Grants, Construction and Regeneration Act 1996

questions.prompt: A payer wants to pay less than the notified sum on a construction contract. What must they do?
questions.answer: Give a valid pay less notice, in time and specifying the sum considered due and the basis of calculation. Absent a valid notice, the notified sum falls due in full.
questions.authority: s111 Housing Grants, Construction and Regeneration Act 1996

questions.prompt: A contractor has not been paid a sum due and no pay less notice was given. Can they stop work?
questions.answer: Yes. After giving the required notice of intention, the payee may suspend performance of any or all obligations until payment, with an entitlement to reasonable costs and an extension of time.
questions.authority: s112 Housing Grants, Construction and Regeneration Act 1996

questions.prompt: What is the general health and safety duty an employer owes its own workers?
questions.answer: To ensure, so far as is reasonably practicable, the health, safety and welfare at work of all employees. It is a qualified but non-delegable general duty.
questions.authority: s2 Health and Safety at Work etc. Act 1974

questions.prompt: A sole trader's work could affect members of the public and other trades on site. What do they owe them?
questions.answer: A duty to conduct the undertaking so as to ensure, so far as is reasonably practicable, that persons not in their employment are not exposed to risks to their health or safety.
questions.authority: s3 Health and Safety at Work etc. Act 1974

questions.prompt: Do workers themselves owe health and safety duties?
questions.answer: Yes. An employee must take reasonable care for their own safety and that of others affected by their acts, and cooperate with the employer so far as needed to comply with duties.
questions.authority: s7 Health and Safety at Work etc. Act 1974

questions.prompt: A tradesperson runs their business through a limited company and is its sole director. What core duty do they owe the company?
questions.answer: To act in the way they consider, in good faith, would be most likely to promote the success of the company for the benefit of its members as a whole, having regard to the listed factors.

questions.topic: Duty of care, skill and diligence
questions.prompt: What standard of care does a company director owe?
questions.answer: Reasonable care, skill and diligence, judged by both an objective standard (what may reasonably be expected of a person in the role) and any higher subjective standard given the director's actual knowledge and experience.

questions.prompt: What is the directors' obligation regarding the company's annual accounts and the registrar?
questions.answer: To deliver the company's annual accounts and reports to the registrar of companies within the statutory filing period, an obligation resting on the directors.

questions.prompt: Besides accounts, what periodic statement must a company deliver to Companies House to confirm its information is up to date?
questions.answer: A confirmation statement, delivered at least once in each review period, confirming that the company information on the register is up to date.

questions.prompt: A director keeps trading when they knew, or ought to have concluded, there was no reasonable prospect of avoiding insolvent liquidation. What is the exposure?
questions.answer: On liquidation, the court may order the director to contribute to the company's assets for wrongful trading, unless they took every step to minimise loss to creditors after that point.

questions.prompt: How does fraudulent trading differ from wrongful trading?
questions.answer: Fraudulent trading requires carrying on business with intent to defraud creditors or for any fraudulent purpose; it carries both civil liability to contribute and criminal liability, and turns on dishonest intent rather than mere unreasonableness.

questions.prompt: Before insolvency a company pays one creditor ahead of others, wishing to prefer them. Can that be unwound?
questions.answer: Yes. A liquidator or administrator may challenge a preference given within the relevant time where the company was influenced by a desire to put that creditor in a better position; the court may restore the position.

questions.prompt: A tradesperson designs a logo and marketing materials. Do they need to register copyright to be protected?
questions.answer: No. Copyright subsists automatically on creation of a qualifying original work recorded in some form; there is no registration system for copyright in the UK.
questions.authority: ss1 and 3 Copyright, Designs and Patents Act 1988

questions.prompt: An employee creates a work in the course of their employment. Who owns the copyright?
questions.answer: The author is first owner as a general rule, but where a work is made by an employee in the course of employment the employer is first owner, subject to any agreement to the contrary.
questions.authority: s11 Copyright, Designs and Patents Act 1988

questions.prompt: How long does copyright in a literary, dramatic, musical or artistic work last?
questions.answer: For the life of the author plus a period of years after death (currently 70), set by the provision.
questions.authority: s12 Copyright, Designs and Patents Act 1988

questions.prompt: Someone knowingly obtains or discloses a customer's personal data without the controller's consent. Is that an offence?
questions.answer: Yes. It is a criminal offence to knowingly or recklessly obtain, disclose or procure the disclosure of personal data without the consent of the controller, subject to defences.

questions.prompt: What is the statutory test for whether a business expense is deductible in computing trading profits?
questions.answer: The expense must be incurred wholly and exclusively for the purposes of the trade; expenditure with a non-trade or dual purpose is disallowed unless a definite part is wholly and exclusively for the trade.
questions.authority: s34 Income Tax (Trading and Other Income) Act 2005
